unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: In this unit, your child learns about water — where it comes from, where we find it, and all the ways we use it every day. They meet rainy and sunny weather, animals that live in water, and the big idea that water is a gift we should not waste. The goal is warm, confident English and a thankful heart for water — not perfect spelling.
unit.learningPath:
Begin with the guided teacher launch and learn a few words through pictures and play.
Listen to the Unit story and join in with repeated language.
Complete six language, speaking, early-writing and practical activities.
Meet your teacher three times each week for two weeks.
Finish the ten-question checkpoint and tell an adult what you can do.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The clear liquid we drink, and that fills rivers and rain.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Samira drinks cool water after school.
practiceSentences: Water helps every plant grow tall.
Is the water hot or cold?
Look, the water is very clear!
I pour water into my cup.
We use water to wash before prayer.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: w - a - t - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say water slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Drops of water that fall down from grey clouds.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Soft rain falls on our window.
practiceSentences: Rain makes the garden green again.
Can you hear the rain outside?
Yes, the rain stopped a moment ago.
I like my boots in the rain.

dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: r - a - i - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say rain slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: One tiny round bit of water, smaller than your fingertip.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: A drop of rain lands on Musa.

Where did that drop of water go?

dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: d - r - o - p
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say drop slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Covered with water, the way your coat feels after rain.

dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say wet slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Having no water on it at all, like sand in sunshine.

dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: Say, tap and trace: d - r - y

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: With no dirt on it, washed and fresh.

dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say clean slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To stay on top of the water without sinking.

dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say float slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To go down under the water instead of staying on top.

dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult, ask the tutor to say whale slowly, use it in one easy sentence and wait for you to repeat.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Say why plants, animals, and people all need water.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Observed through pointing, speaking, drawing, tracing, matching, play and one supported independent response.

outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Observed through pointing, speaking, drawing, tracing, matching, play and one supported independent response.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Say how we use water: “I use water to drink / wash / cook.”
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Observed through pointing, speaking, drawing, tracing, matching, play and one supported independent response.

outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Observed through pointing, speaking, drawing, tracing, matching, play and one supported independent response.

outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Observed through pointing, speaking, drawing, tracing, matching, play and one supported independent response.

outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Observed through pointing, speaking, drawing, tracing, matching, play and one supported independent response.

readings.title: The Well in the Village
readings.theme: water, weather, living things and care
readings.setting: Home, online lesson or Grade 1 classroom
readings.passageScript:
Amal lived in a small village. Near her house there was a deep well. The water in the well was cool and clean.

Every morning, Amal walked to the well with her big brother Ishmael. They carried an empty pot.

“Fill it slowly,” said Ishmael. “Water is precious. We must not waste one drop.”

Amal helped pull the rope. Up came the pot, full and heavy. “We did it!” she said.

At home, Mama used the water. “We use water to cook,” she said, making warm soup.

“And we use water to wash,” said Amal. She washed her hands until they were clean.

“And we use water to drink,” said little Hodan. She drank a big cup and smiled.

One day, the land was very dry. No rain came. The grass was brown. The plants did not grow.

“The well is getting low,” said Ishmael. “We must share and be careful.”

So the whole village shared the water. Nobody wasted a single drop.

Then, one afternoon, the sky went grey. Big clouds came. Drip… drip… drip…

“Rain!” shouted Amal. “It is raining!”

The children ran outside and danced in the rain. The rain filled the river. The rain filled the well.

The brown grass turned green again. The plants began to grow. The animals came to drink.

That night, Amal looked at the full well. “Thank you, Allah, for the water,” she said softly. “Water is wonderful.”

readings.title: Talk about Wonderful Water
readings.theme: water, weather, living things and care
readings.setting: Home, online lesson or Grade 1 classroom
readings.passageScript: Look, point and talk. I use water to ___. It is rainy. A ___ needs water. The ___ floats. We must not waste water. ___ live in water. An adult reads each line while the learner points, repeats and acts.

readings.title: Wonderful Water rhyme
readings.theme: water, weather, living things and care
readings.setting: Home, online lesson or Grade 1 classroom
readings.passageScript:
The “Rainy Day” poem — say it together
Rain on the green grass. / Rain on the tree. / Rain on the houses / But not on me!
Wiggle your fingers like falling rain as you say it. Say it three times — a little louder each time!
“Row, Row, Row Your Boat” — sing and do the actions
Row, row, row your boat, / Gently down the stream. / Merrily, merrily, merrily, merrily, / Life is but a dream.
Sit facing your child, hold hands, and rock back and forth like rowing a boat.

comprehension.question: Who walked to the well with Amal every morning?
comprehension.explanation: Every morning Amal walked to the well with her big brother Ishmael.

comprehension.question: What did Amal and Ishmael carry to the well?
comprehension.explanation: The story says they carried an empty pot to the well.

comprehension.question: Ishmael says water is precious. How much must they not waste?
comprehension.explanation: Ishmael says, 'Water is precious. We must not waste one drop.'

comprehension.question: What happened to the grass when no rain came?
comprehension.explanation: When the land was very dry the grass was brown and the plants did not grow.

comprehension.question: How did Amal feel when the rain came? What did she shout?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Excited and happy: 'Rain! It is raining!'
comprehension.explanation: Amal shouts, 'Rain! It is raining!' and the children dance outside.

comprehension.question: Why did the whole village share the water?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Because the well was getting low
comprehension.explanation: Ishmael says the well is getting low, so everyone shares and is careful.

comprehension.question: Finish this talk line: 'I use water to ___.'
comprehension.correctAnswer: I use water to wash. (any true use)
comprehension.explanation: The first talk line is 'I use water to ___.'

comprehension.question: Which talk line tells us about the weather?
comprehension.explanation: 'It is rainy' is the weather line in the talk lines.

comprehension.question: Say 'A ___ needs water' about a plant.
comprehension.explanation: 'A ___ needs water' is the talk line for living things that need water.

comprehension.question: Which talk line reminds us to look after water?
comprehension.explanation: 'We must not waste water' is the caring line in the talk lines.

comprehension.question: Say the line 'The ___ floats' about a boat.
comprehension.explanation: 'The ___ floats' is the talk line for things that stay on top of water.

comprehension.question: Finish this talk line: '___ live in water.'
comprehension.explanation: The last talk line is '___ live in water,' for animals that live in water.

grammar.title: Say what you use water for
grammar.explanation: Think of one way you use water every day. After 'to', use the plain action word.
grammar.commonMistake: Some children say 'I use water to washing.' After 'to' we use the plain word: 'to wash'.
grammar.memoryTip: Mime the action as you say it: drinking, washing, watering.
grammar.practice: Say three uses: I use water to drink. I use water to wash. I use water to make wudu.

grammar.title: Talk about rainy weather
grammar.explanation: Look outside and say the weather word. Try rainy, sunny, cloudy or windy.
grammar.commonMistake: 'It is rain' names the water, not the weather. The weather word is 'rainy'.
grammar.memoryTip: Weather words often end in -y: rainy, sunny, windy, cloudy.
grammar.practice: Say today's weather three ways: It is rainy. It is cloudy. It is windy.

grammar.title: Say what needs water
grammar.explanation: Name living things that need water to grow and stay well. Plants, animals and people all do.
grammar.commonMistake: 'A plant need water' misses the /s/. When we talk about one thing we say 'needs'.
grammar.memoryTip: One plant needs. Listen for that little /s/ sound at the end.
grammar.practice: Say three: A plant needs water. A camel needs water. A baby needs water.

grammar.title: Say what floats on water
grammar.explanation: Drop something safely into water and watch. If it stays on top, it floats.
grammar.commonMistake: Floating and sinking get mixed up. Floats means it stays on top; sinks means it goes down.
grammar.memoryTip: Float your hand on top of the table, then sink it down low for 'sinks'.
grammar.practice: Test and say three: The leaf floats. The boat floats. The stone sinks.

grammar.title: A rule about saving water
grammar.explanation: Say this rule out loud. 'Must not' tells us something we should never do.
grammar.commonMistake: 'We must not to waste water' has an extra 'to'. After 'must not' we use the plain word: 'waste'.
grammar.memoryTip: Turn an imaginary tap off tightly as you say 'must not waste'.
grammar.practice: Say the rule, then three ways to save: Turn off the tap. Use a small cup. Mend a dripping tap.

grammar.title: Name creatures that live in water
grammar.explanation: Think of animals whose home is water. Say their name first, then 'live in water'.
grammar.commonMistake: 'Fish lives in water' is right for one fish only. For many we say 'Fish live in water'.
grammar.memoryTip: Wiggle your hand like a swimming fish as you say the sentence.
grammar.practice: Name three: Fish live in water. Frogs live in water. Ducks live in water.

speaking.title: Speaking 1 - Listen and point
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Mime a job you need water for, then say it. Say: 'I use water to drink.' 'I use water to wash.' 'I use water for wudu.'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, practise the pattern I use water to ___. Give warm feedback on one clear word and one next try.

speaking.title: Speaking 2 - Repeat the model
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Look outside, then copy the adult's weather sentence. Say: 'It is rainy.' 'It is sunny today.' 'It is windy and cold.'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, practise the pattern It is rainy. Give warm feedback on one clear word and one next try.

speaking.title: Speaking 3 - Ask and answer
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Ask and answer about living things that drink. Say: 'Does a plant need water?' 'Yes, a plant needs water.' 'A goat needs water too.'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, practise the pattern A ___ needs water. Give warm feedback on one clear word and one next try.

speaking.title: Speaking 4 - Picture talk
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Drop things into a bowl of water and report what happens. Say: 'The leaf floats.' 'The stone sinks.' 'The cup floats.'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, practise the pattern The ___ floats. Give warm feedback on one clear word and one next try.

speaking.title: Speaking 5 - Role-play
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Role-play washing your hands carefully with a partner. Say: 'We must not waste water.' 'Turn off the tap.' 'Water is a gift from Allah.'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, practise the pattern We must not waste water. Give warm feedback on one clear word and one next try.

speaking.title: Speaking 6 - My confident turn
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Take your own turn telling the class about water animals. Say: 'Fish live in water.' 'Frogs live in water.' 'A camel drinks a lot of water.'
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, practise the pattern ___ live in water. Give warm feedback on one clear word and one next try.

writing.title: Writing 1 - Draw and tell
writing.promptAndInstructions: Draw one way your family uses water at home. Tell an adult why we must never waste it.
writing.successCriteria: I said the idea first; formed or selected meaningful letters and words; used the model; checked my work with an adult
writing.support: Adult read-aloud, picture choices, tracing dots, movable word cards and oral rehearsal are allowed.
writing.extension: Add a second label or sentence and read it aloud.

writing.title: Writing 2 - Trace
writing.promptAndInstructions: Trace the words 'rain' and 'water'. Draw three raindrops beside each word, then read them both aloud.
writing.successCriteria: I said the idea first; formed or selected meaningful letters and words; used the model; checked my work with an adult
writing.support: Adult read-aloud, picture choices, tracing dots, movable word cards and oral rehearsal are allowed.
writing.extension: Add a second label or sentence and read it aloud.

writing.title: Writing 3 - Match and copy
writing.promptAndInstructions: Put the word cards fish, plant and tree on the right pictures. Copy the one that lives in water.
writing.successCriteria: I said the idea first; formed or selected meaningful letters and words; used the model; checked my work with an adult
writing.support: Adult read-aloud, picture choices, tracing dots, movable word cards and oral rehearsal are allowed.
writing.extension: Add a second label or sentence and read it aloud.

writing.title: Writing 4 - Label
writing.promptAndInstructions: Add two labels to your water drawing: 'tap' and 'cup'. Then tell an adult which one you must turn off.
writing.expectedLength: One label, phrase or short sentence
writing.successCriteria: I said the idea first; formed or selected meaningful letters and words; used the model; checked my work with an adult
writing.support: Adult read-aloud, picture choices, tracing dots, movable word cards and oral rehearsal are allowed.
writing.extension: Add a second label or sentence and read it aloud.

writing.title: Writing 5 - Complete a pattern
writing.promptAndInstructions: Copy the sentence 'We must not waste water.' Write it once, neatly, then draw a tap that is turned off.
writing.expectedLength: One label, phrase or short sentence
writing.successCriteria: I said the idea first; formed or selected meaningful letters and words; used the model; checked my work with an adult
writing.support: Adult read-aloud, picture choices, tracing dots, movable word cards and oral rehearsal are allowed.
writing.extension: Add a second label or sentence and read it aloud.

writing.title: Writing 6 - Create one sentence
writing.promptAndInstructions: Choose a picture of a water animal. Write one sentence: '___ live in water.' Start it with a capital letter.
writing.expectedLength: One label, phrase or short sentence
writing.successCriteria: I said the idea first; formed or selected meaningful letters and words; used the model; checked my work with an adult
writing.support: Adult read-aloud, picture choices, tracing dots, movable word cards and oral rehearsal are allowed.
writing.extension: Add a second label or sentence and read it aloud.

activities.title: Activity 1
activities.instructionsAndItems: Colour the things that use water: the cup, the plant, the washing hands and the pot of soup. Name each one as you colour it.
activities.answerSummary: Accept the correct match, action, picture choice or a relevant supported spoken response.
activities.deliveryMode: Shared online UI with adult or teacher support

activities.title: Activity 2
activities.instructionsAndItems: Draw yourself using water. Then say: 'I use water to ___.' Choose drinking, washing or watering the plants.
activities.answerSummary: Accept the correct match, action, picture choice or a relevant supported spoken response.
activities.deliveryMode: Shared online UI with adult or teacher support

activities.title: Activity 3
activities.instructionsAndItems: Draw a line from each word to its place: rain to the sky, fish to the sea, tap to the kitchen, puddle to the road. Say each word.
activities.answerSummary: Accept the correct match, action, picture choice or a relevant supported spoken response.
activities.deliveryMode: Shared online UI with adult or teacher support

activities.title: Activity 4
activities.instructionsAndItems: Act out the weather. 1. Splash in the rain. 2. Shiver in the wind. 3. Shade your eyes in the sun. Say the weather word each time.
activities.answerSummary: Accept the correct match, action, picture choice or a relevant supported spoken response.
activities.deliveryMode: Shared online UI with adult or teacher support

activities.title: Activity 5
activities.instructionsAndItems: Point to four water animals: fish, frog, duck and crab. Say 'A ___ lives in water' for every one you find.

activities.title: Activity 6
activities.instructionsAndItems: Do a float or sink test. Put a leaf, a stone, a spoon and a cork in water. Sort them into two piles and tell an adult.

assignments.title: My Wonderful Water show-and-tell page
assignments.instructions: Be a water helper. 1) Draw where water comes from: rain, a river, the sea or a well. 2) With a grown-up, drop two safe objects into a bowl to see what floats and what sinks. 3) Label two words: rain, wet, dry or float. 4) Record yourself saying: I use water to wash. It is rainy today. The ___ floats. We must not waste water. 5) Grown-ups, write one way your family saves water.
assignments.submissionType: Drawing or object + labels + short audio/video + reflection

quizzes.question: Which word is a water word?
quizzes.explanation: Rain is water falling from the clouds; a chair, a shoe and a book have nothing to do with water.

quizzes.question: Samira stood outside in the rain. How are her clothes now?
quizzes.explanation: Rain makes clothes wet; dry is the opposite, and hot and loud do not describe rainy clothes.

quizzes.question: Which word means the opposite of wet?
quizzes.explanation: Dry is the opposite of wet; rain, drop and float are all still to do with water.

quizzes.question: A leaf stays on top of the water. What is the leaf doing?
quizzes.explanation: Staying on top is called floating; sinking means going to the bottom.

quizzes.question: Musa drops a heavy stone into a bucket of water. What does it do?
quizzes.explanation: A heavy stone sinks to the bottom; only light things like a leaf float on top.

quizzes.question: Which of these needs water to stay alive?
quizzes.options: a spoon | a plant | a chair | a rock
quizzes.explanation: Plants are living and need water; a rock, a spoon and a chair are not alive.

quizzes.question: What falls from the clouds when it rains?
quizzes.explanation: Rain is drops of water from the clouds; sand, leaves and stones do not fall from clouds.

quizzes.question: What do we use water for before we pray?
quizzes.explanation: We wash with clean water before we pray; writing, reading and sleeping do not need water.

quizzes.question: Which is a good way to save water?
quizzes.options: Turn the tap off while you brush your teeth. | Leave the tap running all day. | Play with the garden hose for hours. | Fill the bath right to the top.
quizzes.correctAnswer: Turn the tap off while you brush your teeth.
quizzes.explanation: Turning the tap off stops water being wasted; the other choices all waste a lot of water.

quizzes.question: Which sentence tells us about the weather?
quizzes.options: I am rainy today. | It is rainy today. | Today rainy is. | The rainy is today.
quizzes.explanation: We say it is for the weather; the other sentences have the words muddled up.

teacherNotes.note: Use 15-20 minute independent blocks inside the lesson. Read every instruction aloud, model before expecting a response, and accept pointing, acting, drawing, tracing or speaking as valid evidence.
A teacher or caregiver remains present whenever the young learner uses recording or AI tutor features. The adult controls the device and protects the child's personal information.

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Answer: Her big brother, Ishmael. Every morning Amal walked to the well with her big brother Ishmael. Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: An empty pot. The story says they carried an empty pot to the well. Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: Not one drop. Ishmael says, 'Water is precious. We must not waste one drop.' Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: It turned brown. When the land was very dry the grass was brown and the plants did not grow. Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: Excited and happy: 'Rain! It is raining!' Amal shouts, 'Rain! It is raining!' and the children dance outside. Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: Because the well was getting low. Ishmael says the well is getting low, so everyone shares and is careful. Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: I use water to wash. (any true use). The first talk line is 'I use water to ___.' Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: It is rainy. 'It is rainy' is the weather line in the talk lines. Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: A plant needs water. 'A ___ needs water' is the talk line for living things that need water. Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: We must not waste water. 'We must not waste water' is the caring line in the talk lines. Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: The boat floats. 'The ___ floats' is the talk line for things that stay on top of water. Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.
Answer: Fish live in water. The last talk line is '___ live in water,' for animals that live in water. Accept the idea in the child's own words, spoken or pointed to.

Accept any correct sentence built on the pattern “I use water to ___.”. Model answers: I use water to drink. I use water to wash. I use water to make wudu. Watch for: Some children say 'I use water to washing.' After 'to' we use the plain word: 'to wash'.

Accept any correct sentence built on the pattern “It is rainy.”. Model answers: It is rainy. It is cloudy. It is windy. Watch for: 'It is rain' names the water, not the weather. The weather word is 'rainy'.

Accept any correct sentence built on the pattern “A ___ needs water.”. Model answers: A plant needs water. A camel needs water. A baby needs water. Watch for: 'A plant need water' misses the /s/. When we talk about one thing we say 'needs'.

Accept any correct sentence built on the pattern “The ___ floats.”. Model answers: The leaf floats. The boat floats. The stone sinks. Watch for: Floating and sinking get mixed up. Floats means it stays on top; sinks means it goes down.

Accept any correct sentence built on the pattern “We must not waste water.”. Model answers: Turn off the tap. Use a small cup. Mend a dripping tap. Watch for: 'We must not to waste water' has an extra 'to'. After 'must not' we use the plain word: 'waste'.

Accept any correct sentence built on the pattern “___ live in water.”. Model answers: Fish live in water. Frogs live in water. Ducks live in water. Watch for: 'Fish lives in water' is right for one fish only. For many we say 'Fish live in water'.

Correct option: floating. Staying on top is called floating; sinking means going to the bottom.
Correct option: sink. A heavy stone sinks to the bottom; only light things like a leaf float on top.
